name: Defence & Trade
status: NATO 2% met. Trade diversification measurable. Accounting caveats noted.
rationale: The government set a specific NATO target, funded it ($81.8B over 5 years), and delivered on timeline. Trade diversification produced measurable results. The A- rather than A reflects accounting caveats (Coast Guard reclassification) and Canada placing in NATO's bottom third at exactly 2.0%. Policy Options flags the 3.5% GDP defence target is unfunded long-term.

promises.evidence: Confirmed by NATO Secretary General annual report. $81.8B committed over five years.
One Canadian Economy Act (Bill C-5) received Royal Assent June 26, 2025 with bipartisan support.
Announced in Budget 2025. Fund structure being designed. No disbursements yet.
Policy framework announced. Implementation underway across departments.
Announced but not yet launched. No timeline confirmed.

perspectives.critics: Policy Options: the 3.5% GDP defence target cannot be financed without tax increases or structural deficits. NDP and labour groups: $81.8B for defence while housing, healthcare, and childcare remain underfunded is a priorities failure, not a win.
perspectives.defenders: Fraser Institute and NATO allies confirm this is the most significant Canadian defence achievement since the Cold War. Trade diversification is structural and measurable — reducing US dependence from 73% to 67% protects Canada regardless of who governs next.

nextTrigger: NATO annual defence spending report (June 2026). CUSMA review (July 2026). Next trade data release.
inherited: Canada had not met NATO 2% since the Cold War. Trade was 73% US-dependent. Interprovincial barriers were decades-old.

defence.label: Defence
defence.rationale: NATO 2% met and confirmed by NATO Secretary General Annual Report. $81.8B committed. Procurement advancing.

trade.label: Trade Diversification
trade.rationale: US export share down to 71.7% (2025 annual). Non-US exports +17.2% (full-year 2025). January 2026 monthly: -6.5% (volatile, one month).

name: Major Projects
status: MPO created with real powers. Credit-claiming exceeds contribution. National interest designation unused.
rationale: The Major Projects Office is a real institutional innovation with potentially transformative powers (Building Canada Act, C-5). However, the $116B headline figure primarily represents pre-existing investment decisions. The national interest designation has never been used. The Fraser Institute and Angus Reid both found the government overstated its role. AI investment and northern strategy are too early to judge. Note: the credit-claiming penalty modifier (-0.3 GPA) is reflected in the current C grade. Without the penalty, the raw assessment would be C+, reduced to C based on documented overclaiming of pre-existing projects by Fraser Institute and Angus Reid.

promises.text: Major Federal Project Office with 2-year decisions
promises.evidence: MPO created and operational. No project has completed the full 2-year cycle yet.

promises.text: One Project, One Review within 6 months
promises.evidence: Aspiration stated but regulatory harmonization not yet achieved across agencies.
Alto concept announced. No construction expected before 2029.
Program expanded to $10B. Operational and accepting applications.

perspectives.critics: Angus Reid and Fraser Institute: the $116B headline is mostly pre-existing investment rebranded. The national interest designation has never been used — the MPO's most powerful tool sits on the shelf. Environmental and Indigenous groups: fast-tracking without consent is not progress.
perspectives.defenders: The Building Canada Act is genuine institutional reform with bipartisan support. The $10B Indigenous Loan Guarantee is operational. The MPO is less than a year old — judging it on unused powers rather than institutional capacity is premature.

nextTrigger: First use of national interest designation. MPO project completion or cancellation. Alto rail feasibility report.
inherited: Regulatory assessments routinely exceeded 5 years. Bill C-69 was criticized across the spectrum. Private investment proceeded despite, not because of, the framework.

name: Fiscal Health
status: Deficit nearly doubled. No credible medium-term anchor. PBO gives 7.5% chance of meeting targets.
rationale: The deficit of $78.3B nearly doubled from the prior year. Fitch issued a renewed warning on AA+ rating. The PBO gives only 7.5% probability the deficit-to-GDP ratio will decline as projected. Defence spending is strategically justified, but the broader fiscal package lacks a credible anchor. GST revenue ($54.4B) is now exceeded by debt servicing ($55.6B). IMF/IFSD note current debt levels are manageable, but the trajectory given defence + capital commitments is unsustainable without tax increases.

promises.evidence: No credible path identified. PBO projects 7.5% probability of meeting deficit targets.
Spending review announced. Departmental cuts being implemented. Broadbent Institute warns of service delivery impacts.
Implemented in Budget 2025.
Reversed in Budget 2025. Fraser Institute acknowledged as step in right direction.

perspectives.critics: C.D. Howe: every GST dollar now goes to debt servicing. PBO gives 7.5% probability of meeting deficit targets — in any other context that plan gets rejected. The Fitch warning is a real signal. La Presse: the gas tax suspension costs $2.4B the government cannot afford.
perspectives.defenders: IMF and IFSD note Canada's debt-to-GDP ratio remains manageable by international standards. Defence spending is strategically necessary during a trade war. The operating/capital budget split is a legitimate framework innovation. Kevin Page: "There is no fiscal crisis."

nextTrigger: April 28, 2026 economic update from Finance Minister Champagne. PBO spring fiscal outlook. Fitch/Moody's review.
inherited: Elevated post-pandemic deficits. Rising debt-servicing costs. Fiscal anchor of reducing debt-to-GDP already abandoned by prior government.

name: Economic Policy Response
status: Structural productivity crisis inherited. AI and minerals investments directionally correct but business investment flat. Grade reflects policy response and trajectory, not inherited conditions.
rationale: This dimension grades the government's policy response to Canada's structural productivity and competitiveness challenges — not the inherited GDP per capita level, which reflects a decade of underinvestment. The AI compute strategy ($2B), critical minerals push ($18.5B), and interprovincial reform are directionally correct. But business investment remains flat for a fourth year, specific barriers (permitting, tax rates, electricity costs) remain unresolved, and the OECD projects Canada last among 38 members. The grade reflects whether the policy direction and scale are adequate to change the trajectory, recognizing that outcomes will take years to materialize.

promises.evidence: Launched Dec 2024. Microsoft committed C$19B. SCALE AI $128.5M round completed.
$18.5B across 56 partnerships confirmed. Canada produces 10 of 12 NATO defence-critical minerals.
Announced but reports not yet public.

perspectives.critics: If Carney is the credibility candidate — the one markets trust — why has business investment not turned after 13 months? Permitting, tax rates, electricity costs, and regulatory uncertainty remain unresolved. OECD projects Canada dead last through 2060.
perspectives.defenders: The AI compute strategy ($2B), critical minerals ($18.5B), and Microsoft's $19B commitment are directionally correct. Productivity is a decade-long structural problem — expecting a 13-month turnaround misunderstands the timeline required.

nextTrigger: March 2026 LFS release. Q1 2026 GDP data. Business investment quarterly report.
inherited: GDP per capita declined 2.0% from 2020-2024. Business investment falling since 2006. R&D at half the US rate. Population-driven growth masked per-capita decline for a decade.

name: Affordability Response
status: Government affordability measures cover <20% of household cost increases. Grocery Code voluntary. Tariff costs add $1,450-$2,000/yr per household. Grade reflects federal policy response, not global price levels.
rationale: This dimension grades the adequacy of the federal government's policy response to household cost pressure — not the price levels themselves, which are driven by global markets, exchange rates, and supply chains largely outside federal control. The $307 grocery benefit covers less than 20% of the estimated annual cost increase. The Grocery Code of Conduct is voluntary. Retaliatory tariffs compound the burden. The grade reflects what the government chose to do about affordability, and whether its interventions match the scale of the problem.

promises.evidence: GST/HST Credit top-up operational. PBO estimates ~$307 incremental per household. Full benefit up to $1,890 for family of four.
Operational Jan 1, 2026. Voluntary framework. Experts broadly aligned it will not lower retail prices.
Policy framework announced. Implementation details pending.

perspectives.critics: NDP: $307 per household against $1,600+ in cost increases is an insult to struggling families. The Grocery Code is voluntary by design — the government chose not to regulate a concentrated industry. Broadbent: this is a wealth transfer from consumers to oligopolies.
perspectives.defenders: Global food markets, exchange rates, and retaliatory tariffs drive most of the price increase — these are largely outside federal control. The consumer carbon tax elimination was a real cost reduction. The benefit reaches the lowest-income households first.

nextTrigger: Next monthly CPI release (StatsCan). Dalhousie 2027 food price forecast. Competition Bureau grocery investigation update.
inherited: Prices already 30%+ above pre-pandemic levels. Highly concentrated retail sector. Competition Bureau investigations underway.

name: Carbon Pricing Policy
status: Consumer carbon price eliminated (affordability win). Industrial OBPS preserved at $95/t headline but ~$20/t effective. Grade assesses the policy handling of both components.
rationale: This dimension grades the government's handling of carbon pricing as a policy instrument — the decision to eliminate the consumer levy, preserve the industrial system, and the coherence of that distinction. The consumer elimination was a genuine affordability win and Day 1 commitment delivered. The industrial system's preservation is defensible on competitiveness grounds. However, CCI found the effective credit price is ~$20/tonne despite a $95 headline, raising questions about whether the industrial system achieves its stated purpose. The grade reflects policy handling quality, not climate outcomes (which are graded under Climate & Environment).

metrics.label: Small biz share of carbon revenue

promises.evidence: Fuel charge rate set to zero effective April 1, 2025. Signed by PM on March 14, 2025.
OBPS maintained and extended to 2035 under Made-in-Canada Competitiveness Strategy.
Concept discussed at Davos. No legislation or framework published.

perspectives.critics: Canadian Climate Institute: industrial credits trade at ~$20/tonne despite a $95 headline price — the "preserved" system is largely performative. NDP: eliminating the consumer rebate hurt low-income households who received more in rebates than they paid in carbon costs.
perspectives.defenders: The consumer tax had become a political liability undermining public support for any climate action. Preserving the industrial system maintains the investment signal for large emitters while removing household burden. Politically and economically defensible.

nextTrigger: OBPS tightening schedule. Carbon border adjustment proposal. CCI next assessment of effective vs headline price.
inherited: Consumer fuel charge at $80/tonne, on track for $170 by 2030. Industrial OBPS operating alongside. Both politically and economically contentious.

name: Climate & Environment
status: One of largest environmental policy reversals in Canadian history. No replacement strategy. CCI confirms Canada on track for half of required 2030 reduction. See Carbon Pricing Policy for industrial pricing details.
rationale: The Canadian Climate Institute’s Feb 2026 assessment found Canada on track for 18-22% emissions reduction by 2030, against a 40-45% target. The PM acknowledged targets will be missed. ECCC is losing 840+ positions and $1.3B in budget. The emissions cap, EV mandate, Greener Homes Grant, and tree-planting program were eliminated or halved. The trade-war context provides partial justification, but the scale goes beyond tactical adjustment. The gas tax suspension (April 14, $2.4B cost) adds further pressure.

promises.evidence: Concept announced. No construction timeline or funding structure confirmed.
$3.8B recommitted for 30x30 target (March 2026). Marine target will fall short at 28%.
PM acknowledged 2030 and 2035 targets will be missed. No revised pathway published.

PM publicly acknowledged to Radio-Canada that Canada will miss both targets. CCI projects roughly half of required reduction. Targets not formally withdrawn but acknowledged as unachievable under current policy.

perspectives.critics: Canadian Climate Institute: Canada is on track for half the required 2030 emissions reduction. PM himself acknowledged targets will be missed. ECCC losing 840+ positions. No replacement strategy exists. National Observer: "the fight against climate crisis has disappeared from Ottawa's radar."
perspectives.defenders: The trade-war context is real — maintaining the full climate framework during tariff escalation would have compounded economic pressure. The 30x30 conservation commitment ($3.8B) is genuine. Industrial carbon pricing is preserved. 79% of electricity is already low-carbon.

nextTrigger: Government response to CCI February 2026 assessment. Any new climate plan or replacement strategy. ECCC budget execution.
inherited: Most comprehensive climate framework in Canadian history, but already off-track on 2030 targets. Trade war creating genuine economic pressure.

name: Immigration
status: Sharpest immigration contraction in modern history. Correction is substantial and necessary. Long-term model not yet defined (tracked as next grade trigger).
rationale: The government executed the sharpest immigration contraction in modern Canadian history. This addresses a real absorption failure — housing, healthcare, and labour market capacity were overwhelmed. However, the correction creates downside risks (healthcare staffing, agriculture, higher ed) The right-sized long-term model has not been defined, which is tracked as the primary trigger for the next grade movement rather than a current grading input. The Toronto Star frames this as cautious stabilization rather than bold reform.

promises.evidence: 380,000 PR target for 2026-2028 = ~0.95% of population. Confirmed in November 2025 levels plan.

promises.text: Temp residents below 5% by end of 2027
promises.evidence: Non-permanent residents dropped from 3.15M (7.6%) to 2.68M. On track but not yet at 5%.
Student permits down 60%. TFW program tightened. Asylum stream reforms pending.

perspectives.critics: Toronto Star and healthcare groups: the sharp pullback creates real staffing shortfalls in hospitals, long-term care, and agriculture. Higher education faces revenue collapse. NDP: temporary workers were exploited under the old system — the fix should protect workers, not just cut numbers.
perspectives.defenders: The Bank of Canada found the surge suppressed wages by 0.7% and widened the wage gap to -22.6%. Housing and healthcare were overwhelmed. Even the Fraser Institute acknowledges the correction was necessary. Population declined for the first time since Confederation — the pressure valve is working.

nextTrigger: Next quarterly population estimate (StatsCan). IRCC monthly data. Healthcare staffing data showing pullback impact.
inherited: Temporary residents peaked at 3.15M (7.6% of population). Housing, healthcare, and labour absorption overwhelmed. Wage suppression documented by Bank of Canada.

name: Housing Supply
status: 4,000 units announced vs. hundreds of thousands needed. Ontario deal is real but scale insufficient.
rationale: Housing delivery remains a fraction of identified need. Build Canada Homes announced 4,000 units (construction not yet underway) against a shortfall of hundreds of thousands. The Ontario partnership ($8.8B) targets a real barrier (development charges) but is spread over a decade. Federal housing spending declines 56% by 2028-29. This is leaning toward a proved failure, not merely too early to judge. Policy Options notes supply alone won’t solve the structural affordability problem.

promises.evidence: 259,028 starts in 2025 vs. 450,000+ needed. PBO projects BCH delivers 26,000 units over 5 years (3.7% of shortfall).
4,000 units announced across 6 sites. Construction not yet underway as of April 2026.
$8.8B over 10 years. Development charges cut up to 50% for 3 years. HST removed on homes under $1M for 1 year. Expert Mike Moffatt called it a meaningful step.
Program announced in Budget 2025. Enrollment data not yet available.

perspectives.critics: NDP: where is the public housing? Build Canada Homes is 3.7% of the shortfall. Federal spending is declining 56% by 2028-29. The Tyee: Vancouver increased housing stock 200% while prices soared — supply alone does not deliver affordability. This is a proved failure, not "too early to judge."
perspectives.defenders: The Ontario partnership targets a real barrier (development charges) with real money ($8.8B). Expert Mike Moffatt called it meaningful. Housing is a shared-jurisdiction problem — the federal government cannot unilaterally override municipal zoning or provincial building codes.

nextTrigger: Next CMHC monthly starts release. Build Canada Homes construction commencement. Ontario partnership first results.
inherited: 430-480K annual starts needed, ~245K actual. Construction insolvencies highest of any sector. Development charges up 1,000% in some municipalities.

name: Ethics & Transparency
status: PROBATION. PM ethics framework incomplete relative to novel background. No proven wrongdoing. Disclosure gap unresolved. Whole-letter grade only — plus/minus precision not supported by evidence.
rationale: No public evidence demonstrates self-dealing. This dimension grades the adequacy of the PM’s ethics framework relative to the novel disclosure requirements of his background. The framework is assessed as a single construct encompassing disclosure completeness, conflict screening, and independent review — not as separate items. Carney held ~$6.8M USD in unexercised Brookfield options. The Ethics Commissioner has not published a detailed review. Democracy Watch describes the ethics screen as “loophole-filled.” The disclosure standard for a PM with this background should include full public accounting reviewed independently.

promises.evidence: Ethics screen and blind trust established. Democracy Watch and House Ethics Committee have raised concerns about completeness.
No published independent review of the structural conflict between career path and policy decisions.

perspectives.critics: Democracy Watch: the ethics screen is "loophole-filled." Carney held ~$6.8M in unexercised Brookfield options. The structural alignment between his career (TCFD, GFANZ, Brookfield) and preserving industrial carbon pricing is the definition of a perception conflict. Why has this not been resolved proactively?
perspectives.defenders: No public evidence demonstrates self-dealing. The frameworks Carney helped build (carbon pricing, ESG disclosure) are mainstream G7 consensus, not one individual's agenda. The industrial carbon system would likely exist under any PM. The Ethics Commissioner has not found wrongdoing.

nextTrigger: Ethics Commissioner publication of Brookfield review. Any new disclosure of retained financial interests. House Ethics Committee report.
inherited: No prior PM had Carney's specific background in carbon market architecture and transition finance. Novel disclosure challenge without clear precedent.

name: Flagship Delivery
status: PROBATION. Measures delivery capacity across 5 flagship files. Strong on defence. Weak on housing and climate. Whole-letter grade only — combination rule determines grade mechanically.
rationale: This dimension measures the government's cross-cutting capacity to convert announcements into measurable results across its five highest-profile files: defence (NATO 2%), housing (Build Canada Homes), major projects (MPO), climate (policy framework), and immigration (levels reduction). It is deliberately narrow — it grades execution machinery, not policy merit. Defence shows the government can deliver when focused. Housing and climate show it cannot yet deliver at scale on domestic files. The 40,000 public service job cuts while pursuing an ambitious agenda raise structural capacity questions.

promises.evidence: MPO framework established. No project has completed the full approval cycle yet.
National interest designation unused. Most referred projects were already in motion. Federalism coordination gaps documented by Policy Options.

perspectives.critics: Policy Options: cooperative federalism is failing — BC's Premier was blindsided by a pipeline deal, Ontario got hours of notice. NDP and PSAC: you cannot cut 40,000 public service positions while pursuing the most ambitious delivery agenda in years. The state literally cannot execute what the government announces.
perspectives.defenders: Defence and trade show genuine follow-through — NATO 2%, trade diversification, One Canadian Economy Act. The government has demonstrated it can execute when focused. 11 of 26 bills passed in 13 months under minority conditions. The majority secured April 13 should accelerate the domestic agenda.

nextTrigger: Post-majority legislative pace (bills passed per month). First Ministers Meeting outcomes. April 28 economic update implementation.
inherited: Federal delivery capacity had been declining. Procurement timelines among slowest in OECD. Multiple program delivery failures documented in prior government.

name: Promise Delivery
status: Standalone accountability tracker. Not included in GPA. Carney government has delivered on defence, trade, and key reversals. Housing and climate commitments fall short.
rationale: The Carney government made specific commitments on defence (NATO 2%), trade diversification, carbon tax elimination, immigration reduction, and interprovincial reform — and delivered on all of them. These are genuine achievements. However, housing targets have been scaled back, climate commitments were largely reversed rather than delivered, and the pattern across domestic files is consistent: ambitious announcements followed by results that fall short. The grade reflects real delivery on priority files alongside persistent underdelivery on household-impact files. Note: Promise Delivery is a derivative dimension. Grade movements that are already captured in their home dimensions (e.g., housing delivery moving Housing Supply) should not independently move this grade. This dimension tracks the cumulative pattern of commitment fulfillment as a political accountability measure, not as an independent policy performance indicator.

sources.label: Angus Reid — Carney government report card

promises.evidence: Day 1 commitment. Fuel charge set to zero April 1, 2025.
Reversed in Budget 2025. Removed disinvestment incentive.
PR targets cut to 380,000. Temp arrivals down 53%. Population declined for first time since Confederation.
One Canadian Economy Act passed with bipartisan support. Genuine structural reform.
Effectively suspended. Guilbeault resigned from cabinet over the pipeline MOU.
Repealed. Limited connection to trade competitiveness rationale.
Program halved. Tangential connection to trade-war justification.

perspectives.critics: NDP and environmental groups: the climate rollbacks (emissions cap, EV mandate, tree planting) are abandoned commitments dressed as pragmatism. Several have no connection to trade competitiveness. Housing targets have been quietly scaled back. Rhetoric consistently outpaces results.
perspectives.defenders: Fraser Institute acknowledges the consumer carbon tax elimination, capital gains reversal, immigration correction, and interprovincial reform as genuine steps in the right direction. Defence delivery is real and verified by NATO. About half the reversals are supported across the political spectrum.

nextTrigger: Next major policy announcement or delivery milestone. Housing starts trajectory. Climate replacement strategy if any.
inherited: 15 major Trudeau-era policies inherited, several already controversial. Election framed as sovereignty referendum, creating specific mandate expectations.
